items.title: Launch HN: TesterArmy (YC P26) – Agents that test web and mobile apps
items.why_it_matters: A YC-backed team launched TesterArmy, an agentic platform that uses AI to run end-to-end tests on web and mobile apps. This is a real product being shipped by a startup, showing what's possible with AI agents in a practical application. You could try using TesterArmy to define and run tests for your AI-ingest project's frontend or backend this week.

items.title: Claude Code now supports artifacts
items.why_it_matters: Anthropic has updated Claude Code to support 'artifacts,' which means the AI can now produce structured outputs like code snippets, JSON, or markdown files directly within your workspace. This makes it easier to integrate AI-generated code and data into your projects. You could experiment with asking Claude Code to generate specific file types or structured data for your AI-ingest project and see how artifacts streamline your workflow this week.

items.title: CLI That Enforces Spec-Driven Development with Claude Code, OpenCode, and Codex
items.why_it_matters: A new CLI tool has been released that helps enforce spec-driven development when working with AI coding tools like Claude Code and Codex. This means you can define your project's specifications, and the tool helps ensure the AI-generated code adheres to them, which can improve your 'direction' skills. You could download this CLI and try using it to guide Claude Code in building a new feature or component for your AI-ingest project this week.

items.title: Show HN: Pi Extension to Maximize AI credits across Cursor, Codex, ClaudeCode
items.why_it_matters: A new Pi extension helps you manage and maximize your AI credits across various coding agents, including Claude Code and Codex, which you use. This could lead to significant cost savings and more efficient use of your AI coding budget, aligning with your interest in saving tokens. You could install this Pi extension and monitor your AI credit usage across your tools this week to understand your spending better.

items.title: Save tokens with Claude Code with this plugin
items.why_it_matters: A new plugin has been released specifically to help you save tokens when using Claude Code. This directly addresses the cost efficiency of your primary AI coding tool, which was a strong positive signal in your past ratings. You could integrate this plugin into your Claude Code setup and observe its impact on your token usage and costs for your AI-ingest project this week.

items.title: Memanto; open-source memory agent that remembers, recalls and answers
items.why_it_matters: Memanto is an open-source memory agent designed to remember, recall, and answer questions. Building robust memory into AI agents is a key challenge, and this tool provides a ready-made solution that could be useful for your AI-ingest project if you want to add more intelligent filtering or interaction. You could clone the Memanto repository and experiment with integrating its memory capabilities into a small part of your AI-ingest backend this week.

items.title: Git platform built for agentic era
items.why_it_matters: A new Git platform called Gitlawb is emerging, specifically designed for the 'agentic era,' aiming to simplify version control for projects heavily involving AI agents. Since you're just learning Git and GitHub properly, exploring a platform built for AI workflows could offer a more intuitive experience for managing your AI-generated code. You could sign up for Gitlawb and try migrating a small part of your AI-ingest project or a new feature branch to it this week to see how it compares to GitHub.

items.title: Show HN: 10x better performance from the Coding Harnesses with LLM-wiki
items.why_it_matters: LLM-wiki claims to offer 10x better performance from 'Coding Harnesses,' which are tools that help LLMs generate better code. Since you rely on Claude Code and Codex for keyboard work, improving their performance could make your 'direction' more efficient and effective. You could visit LLM-wiki.net and explore how to apply their techniques or integrate their tools to enhance the code generation quality for your AI-ingest project this week.

items.title: Layr – a modular UX and product constraint system for AI-built interfaces
items.why_it_matters: Layr is an open-source modular UX and product constraint system designed for interfaces built with AI. As you're building the frontend for AI-ingest with Next.js, a system like Layr could help you structure your UI and ensure consistency, especially when AI is generating parts of it. You could explore the Layr GitHub repository and try setting up a basic component or page for your AI-ingest frontend using its modular system this week.

items.title: You now have cursor-like diff control over Claude Code
items.why_it_matters: A new tool called Norrin provides cursor-like diff control for Claude Code, allowing you to accept or reject functions, track file changes, and see inline diffs. This gives you more granular control over the AI's output, which is crucial for getting good at the 'direction' part of vibe coding. You could integrate Norrin into your Claude Code workflow and use it to review and refine the AI-generated code for your AI-ingest project this week.

items.title: clawmark: open-source CLAUDE.md A/B Testing CLI tool
items.why_it_matters: Clawmark is an open-source CLI tool for A/B testing prompts and outputs with Claude. This is directly relevant to your AI-ingest project, as you'll likely want to optimize how Claude filters news. A/B testing helps you make data-driven decisions for shipping better features. You could download and set up Clawmark to A/B test different Claude prompts for your AI-ingest filtering logic this week.

items.title: Show HN: SDK for embedding zot coding agent in your Node.js applications
items.why_it_matters: A new JavaScript SDK allows you to embed the 'zot coding agent' directly into your Node.js applications. Since your AI-ingest project uses Next.js (which is Node.js-based), this could be a way to integrate more advanced AI coding capabilities or agentic features directly into your application's backend. You could install this SDK and try embedding a simple zot agent function into your AI-ingest backend to automate a small task this week.

items.title: Show HN: OSymandias – Open-source runtime for multi-agent AI systems
items.why_it_matters: OSymandias is an open-source runtime for building and managing multi-agent AI systems. As you're building AI-ingest, you might eventually want to coordinate multiple AI agents for different tasks (e.g., one for filtering, one for summarizing). This runtime provides a framework to do that. You could clone the OSymandias repository and try setting up a basic multi-agent system to handle a simple workflow within your AI-ingest project this week.

items.title: Well-– keep Claude Code's context lean as your codebase grows
items.why_it_matters: A new tool called 'Well-' (from Banyan Outcomes) helps keep Claude Code's context lean, which is important as your codebase grows to prevent the AI from getting overwhelmed or expensive. This directly addresses a practical challenge you'll face as your AI-ingest project expands. You could integrate 'Well-' into your development environment and see how it helps manage context when working on larger files or features in your AI-ingest project this week.

items.title: Show HN: Iamspeed.dev – Fast.com Style but for LLMs
items.why_it_matters: Iamspeed.dev is a simple tool that lets you test the speed of various LLM APIs, similar to how Fast.com tests internet speed. As you use Claude and potentially other AI APIs, monitoring their performance can help you identify slowdowns or choose the fastest option for different tasks. You could use Iamspeed.dev to benchmark the response times of Claude and any other AI APIs you're considering for your AI-ingest project this week.
